planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; space 12–18 inches apart for quick coverage.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, avoiding heavy clay that stays waterlogged in winter; spotted deadnettle forms a quick-spreading mat of silver-marked foliage that suppresses weeds effectively under trees and shrubs. The small pink, white, or purple flowers appear in late spring and continue sporadically through summer. Cut plants back hard in midsummer if they become leggy or stressed by heat to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. The silver-splashed leaves of popular cultivars like 'White Nancy' and 'Beacon Silver' brighten dark corners more effectively than most groundcovers. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted runners in spring or fall; plants spread by stolons and will fill in quickly — space 6–12 inches apart for coverage within one growing season.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, though ajuga tolerates more sun in cooler climates; it spreads by surface stolons to form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet topped by spikes of blue-violet flowers in spring. The low, rosette foliage comes in a range of colors from deep burgundy-bronze ('Caitlin's Giant', 'Burgundy Glow') to chocolate-purple, making it one of the most ornamentally versatile shade groundcovers. In hot, humid climates, crown rot can strike dense stands — improve air circulation by thinning plants if rot appears. Mow lightly after flowering to remove old spikes and encourage a tidier rosette. Spreads aggressively; install edging to keep it out of lawns.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions 6–12 inches apart in spring or early fall; water well until established. Mulch lightly between plants until coverage is complete.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; pachysandra is the standard choice for dense shade under shallow-rooted trees like maples where most plants fail. The glossy, evergreen whorls of foliage form a uniform 6–8 inch carpet that requires essentially no maintenance once established. Small white flowers in spring are mildly fragrant. Volutella blight can cause patches to brown and die — improve drainage, avoid overwatering, and remove affected plant material promptly. Do not plant in full sun or very dry soil, as foliage will yellow and decline rapidly. Takes 2–3 years from planting to achieve full coverage.

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or divisions 12–18 inches apart in spring or fall; stems root wherever they contact soil, filling in gaps quickly once plants establish.

directions: Plant in almost any soil in partial to deep shade; lesser periwinkle is one of the toughest shade groundcovers, tolerating dry shade under mature trees once established. The trailing stems root at nodes to form a weed-suppressing mat studded with periwinkle-blue pinwheel flowers in spring, with scattered repeat bloom into fall. Variegated forms such as 'Illumination' and 'Ralph Shugert' brighten dark spaces. Shear plants back by half in early spring to maintain density and remove dead or straggling stems. Note that Vinca minor can spread into natural areas — consider native alternatives (Pachysandra procumbens, Asarum canadense) in gardens bordering woodlands.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; avoid planting too deeply — the crown should rest at soil level.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, mimicking its native habitat on the forest floor; foamflower produces mounded clumps of deeply lobed, often burgundy-marked foliage and sends up airy white flower spikes in mid-spring that give the plant its common name. Running forms spread by stolons to colonize an area; clump-forming varieties stay tighter. Modern cultivars (many under the Heucherella group when crossed with Heuchera) offer extraordinary foliage variation. Pair with ferns, hostas, and wild ginger for a classic woodland floor combination. Plants dislike summer drought — mulch to retain moisture and site in a spot with consistent moisture.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally 1–2 inches deep in spring before or just as growth emerges; space 18 inches apart. Fall planting is also effective where winters are mild.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal is one of the most graceful native woodland perennials, producing arching stems lined with paired oval leaves and pendant white bell-shaped flowers in late spring that hang below the foliage like small lanterns. Bluish-black berries follow in late summer and are highly attractive to birds. The foliage turns clear yellow in autumn before the stems die back completely. Plants spread slowly by rhizome to form dignified colonies; transplant divisions in spring or fall. Giant Solomon's seal (P. commutatum) grows to 5–6 feet and is equally effective in larger shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in spring; the rhizome is longer than Solomon's seal and should be laid horizontally in a trench rather than planted vertically.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal closely resembles true Solomon's seal in arching habit and foliage, but differs by bearing a plume of creamy white flowers at the stem tip in late spring rather than pendant bells along the stem. Ruby-red berries follow in late summer, providing important food for migratory thrushes and other birds before turning speckled red-purple at maturity. The architectural arching stems are ideal for naturalized woodland plantings combined with ferns, hostas, and trilliums. Plants spread slowly by thick horizontal rhizomes to form colonies; division every 4–5 years maintains vigor.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed immediately after collecting in late summer, or purchase bulbs and transplant in fall; seed requires a warm period followed by cold stratification and will not germinate until the second spring. Growing from bulbs is far faster, yielding harvestable plants in 3–5 years vs. 5–7 from seed.

directions: Plant in deep, rich, moist, humus-laden soil under deciduous hardwoods; ramps are spring ephemerals of the eastern North American forest floor, emerging early when trees are still bare before going fully dormant by midsummer. Both the broad, elliptic leaves and the pungent garlicky-oniony bulbs are edible and among the most sought-after wild foods in Appalachian cuisine. Harvest no more than one leaf per plant, leaving the bulb to regenerate; never harvest more than 10% of any patch to prevent depletion. Plants are notoriously slow from seed — 5–7 years to first harvest — but once established form long-lived, self-seeding colonies. Purchase ethically sourced bulbs and replant promptly in autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for winter and spring harvest in mild climates; in cold climates, sow in late summer or very early spring as soon as soil can be worked. Seed germinates best in cool, moist soil and will not germinate when temperatures exceed 65°F.

directions: Direct sow thickly on the surface of moist, rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; miner's lettuce is a cool-season annual native to western North America that earned its name from Gold Rush miners who ate it to prevent scurvy. The rounded, succulent leaves are mild, slightly sweet, and excellent in salads or as a wilted green. In mild climates (zones 8–10) it thrives as a winter and early spring salad green, tolerating light frost. Plants mature quickly — begin harvesting whole stems at 40 days — and self-seed prolifically once established, often creating a dependable self-renewing patch. Succession-sow every 3 weeks in fall for continuous harvest through spring; plants bolt and die as temperatures exceed 70°F.
sources: University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall or transplant tiny corms 2–3 inches deep in autumn; plants require winter cold to break dormancy and will bloom the following spring. Once established, they self-seed to naturalize freely.

directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees; Virginia spring beauty is a spring ephemeral wildflower that carpets the woodland floor with pink-striped white flowers in early spring before tree canopies close. The narrow, fleshy leaves and small corms are edible — the corms taste like a mild potato and were gathered by Indigenous peoples of eastern North America. Plants go fully dormant by late spring, leaving bare soil where they grew; interplant with ferns, hostas, or other summer-active groundcovers to fill the gap. Self-seeds freely once naturalized — scatter seed right after it ripens in late spring to establish new colonies.
